title: Project Extent
text:
The ecosystem typology for the Project Extent was determined differently than the instructions of the Nature Framework. This is a project deviation that was needed to accurately define the ecosystem typology in the Project Extent. When using the IUCN GET level 3 classifications and tool for the Project Extent (part of the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico), the data was inaccurate and deficient. For instance, the main function groups that were found in the Project Extent included intensive landscapes like annual croplands, plantations, and urban and industrial ecosystems.


Link CSV File Here


However, these functional groups are not true for the Project Extent, as Terra Global has already worked in this area since 2022 and conducted ground truthing. These areas are primarily forested and many of the ejidos included in the program are in the buffer zone of the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve, a largely intact Mayan forest remnant home to much of the wildlife and one of the largest populations of jaguars left in the Yucatan peninsula.
Thus, the data used to define the ecosystem types of the Project Extent was based on research and literature (Jung, et al., 2020). The data was based on the IUCN Global Database that was later created and used for the methodology, so it is still appropriate to use in this case. This data also allowed the program to refine the Project Extent to the 9 ejidos that are included in the Program and it provided much more accurate results.


Link SHP File Here


The main functional group that was found in all of the ejidos was the Tropical/Sub-tropical Dry forests and thickets. While there were several ecosystem types found in each ejido (Map 1), these ecosystem types were excluded due to being arable land, plantations, or urban areas that are not eligible under the methodology. Furthermore, other ecosystem types, like wetlands and sub-tropical/tropical swamps only made up 1.44% and 0.78% respectively, in the largest areas of the ejidos. The CSV file for the different ecosystem types per ejido can also be found here.


Link CSV File Here


This file also contains how the other functional groups were excluded and the total number of dry forest per ejido was summed (find explanation in Project Extent Calculations).

Project Spatial Boundary





Map 1. IUCN Functional Groups (Ecosystem Types) found in the Project Boundary



Project Extent Calculations
While dry savanna make up part of the ecosystem types for some ejidos, it was ultimately excluded from the Project Extent, as program activities are centered to be implemented in the dry forest ecosystems, and any ecosystem that makes up less than 35% of an ejido is excluded due to their limited spatial extent, which reduces their influence on landscape-scale ecological processes and biodiversity outcomes. Prioritizing ecosystem types that represent a more substantial portion of the Project Extent allows for more efficient allocation of resources, more robust monitoring, and greater potential for measurable impact. However, dry savannas are native ecosystems in the project extent and future instances will include this ecosystem.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
text:
Composition Indicators
The five indicators that were chosen were based on the data available from the baseline assessment of the area done for the corresponding VCS/CCB Project (Project ID 3479 IFM V0010). These are also important indicators for overall ecosystem health and function. The composition indicators that are used include 1) mammal species richness, 2) avian species richness, and 3) herpetofauna (amphibians and reptiles) species richness. The structure indicators that are used for the quantification include 4) aboveground biomass and 5) canopy cover, both suitable indicators to inform on ecosystem health and function.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
text:
Mammal Species Richness
Mammal species richness is an appropriate indicator of biodiversity in sub-tropical dry forests as it is sensitive to habitat structure, resource availability, and anthropogenic pressures. Mammals occupy diverse ecological niches, and their presence often reflects overall ecosystem integrity, which serves as an effective proxy for measuring impacts of program activities on the environment (Ceballos and Ehrlich 2002). Additionally, mammals are relatively easy to monitor through camera traps and transect surveys, which are easily replicable for monitoring over time, even in remote areas. Species richness is a simple analysis that can be done by basic training if communities are to take over on biodiversity monitoring.
Mammals were primarily monitored using camera traps that were placed in each ejido. The following pre-approved Standard Operating Procedure (SOP) was used in the nine ejidos. 10 Browing® Dark Ops APEX 20MP brand camera traps or similar equipment was placed, except for those ejidos in which there were logistics problems in the field and only 5 or 6 cameras were placed. Each camera trap was placed in a site with natural vegetation coverage and preferably at the intersection of 2 to 3 trails, they were attached to the base of the trunks at a height of 40 cm from the ground. The cameras were set to take a photo and a 15-second video, they remained active 24 hours a day for the duration of the study (2 weeks).


Avian Species Richness
Bird species richness is an appropriate indicator for sub-tropical dry forests due to birds’ ecological sensitivity, habitat specificity, and functional diversity. The dry forests in the Project Extent are characterized by seasonal rainfall, deciduous vegetation, and high biodiversity, which allows for a wide array of bird species. Many of these species are habitat specialists or migratory species that rely on highly intact structure and seasonal resources. Birds respond quickly to changes in vegetation cover, forest structure and water availability, making them reliable early indicators of ecosystem health.
Program activities are aimed at improving forest structure through corridor development, conservation efforts, and increasing water availability in response to climate change. Changes in species richness over the program lifetime could be reliably attributed to changes due to program activities. Avian species were monitored with point counts along transects during the two week-long baseline biodiversity assessment conducted in 2023 using the pre-approved SOP. The following procedure was used to collect observations of avian species. The counting method was used by transects separated by 1 km between each transect. The criterion for choosing the transects was the proximity of the points where the camera traps were installed but separated at the beginning between 200 and 300 meters, so that the noise from the installation of the cameras would not affect the bird recording. The observation points were separated from each other every 100 m, at each point all the birds observed were recorded for 10 minutes.


Herpetofauna Species Richness
Amphibians and reptiles are highly sensitive to changes in the environment. Reptiles (e.g., lizards, snakes, and turtles) are adapted to the warm, seasonally dry conditions characteristic of these forests. Many species are habitat specialists, occupying niches tied to particular vegetation types, ground cover, or thermal environments. Their population dynamics and community composition can change in response to alterations in land use, canopy cover, or fire regimes, making them effective indicators of habitat quality and connectivity. Amphibians, while generally associated with moist habitats, are also important in dry forests — especially those that rely on ephemeral water sources for reproduction. Their permeable skin makes them vulnerable to changes in temperature, humidity, water availability, and pollutants.
As a result, amphibians provide early warning signs of ecosystem degradation, particularly regarding hydrological balance and microhabitat integrity. Monitoring the richness of both groups captures complementary aspects of ecosystem health: reptiles reflect structural and thermal habitat features, while amphibians respond to moisture dynamics and microhabitat quality. Additionally, these taxa are often underrepresented in conservation assessments, so their inclusion supports a more holistic and taxonomically inclusive biodiversity strategy. It is important to note that monitoring did not occur during the wet season, when herpetofauna richness and diversity would be at its highest. Unfortunately, due to the remoteness of most of the ejidos and the transects, it is nearly impossible to navigate to some of them in the wet season. While it is not expected to observe the highest species richness each monitoring period, it is expected to see moderate richness and will provide a good indication of ecosystem health even in the dry season, when climate may be most impactful on the environment.
The following procedure, which is included in the pre-approved SOP for the Program, was used to monitor the herpetofauna species in the Project Extent. Transects of 1000 meters in length and 5 meters in width was chosen, during which a thorough search was carried out in all the microhabitats present within the transect, where the presence of herpetofauna was considered probable (under stones, under logs, in tree cavities, between tree bark, in burrows in the ground, etc.). The number of transects in each ejido varied depending on its area; the Program sought to cover the greatest heterogeneity of the landscape present in each locality. The lizards were captured using thick rubber bands, throwing them by stretching them with a finger and oriented towards the individual, so that when hitting them they produce a temporary immobilization that allows their capture without causing damage to the specimen.
The study was complemented by reviewing water sources and wells in those locations where there were bodies of water. The amphibians and reptiles found were identified to species level using specialized field guides and were recorded in a field log, along with the number of the specimen, the ejido where it was found, the number of the transect and any characteristic or observation of importance for this monitoring.

title: Step 2. Condition indicators
section: Structure Indicators
text:
Structure Indicators

Aboveground biomass
Aboveground biomass is a widely used indicator for ecosystem structure, carbon storage, and habitat quality in subtropical dry forests. Aboveground biomass reflects the accumulation of woody materials (i.e., trees, shrubs), which influence the ecological functions and services of the ecosystem. In dry forest ecosystems, changes in AGB are related to land-use practices such as deforestation, grazing, and fuelwood extraction. Thus, monitoring AGB helps detect degradation and recovery over time, providing insight into the effectiveness of program activities (i.e., Assisted Native Regeneration in wildlife corridors, improved forest management). AGB is quantifiable through standardized field measurements and remote sensing, making it a practical and scalable indicator for monitoring ecosystem condition across large areas and over time.


Canopy Cover
Canopy cover is a suitable indicator for subtropical dry forests because it reflects forest condition, habitat quality, and restoration progress. It influences microclimate, soil moisture, and biodiversity, particularly for forest-dependent species—and can be easily monitored using remote sensing or field tools. Its sensitivity to disturbance and recovery makes it a reliable measure of ecosystem health.




Ceballos, Gerardo, and Paul R. Ehrlich. 2002. “Mammal Population Losses and the Extinction Crisis.” Science 296 (5569): 904–7. https://doi.org/10.1126/science.1069349.

title: Step 3. Define reference values for Condition indicators
text:
Composition Indicators
Evidence of the documented inventory used to establish reference values for Condition indicators is maintained through a structured compilation of peer-reviewed literature, spatial datasets, and reproducible analytical workflows. All data sources, selection criteria, and analytical steps were recorded and archived to ensure transparency, traceability, and auditability in accordance with Verra requirements.
For biodiversity composition indicators, including mammal, bird, and herpetofauna species richness, the documented inventory consists of peer-reviewed scientific studies published from 2000 onward and primarily conducted in the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve (CBR) and the broader Yucatan Peninsula. The CBR was selected as the primary reference area because it represents near-pristine ecological conditions where most anthropogenic activities are prohibited. Literature searches were conducted systematically using Google Scholar with predefined keyword combinations such as “species richness,” “diversity,” “Calakmul Biosphere Reserve,” and “Yucatan Peninsula,” tailored to each taxonomic group. Records of search terms, publication dates, and inclusion criteria were maintained as part of the inventory documentation.

title: Step 4. Measure Condition indicators at project start
text:
Mammal Species Richness
While the project does have historical start date, biodiversity monitoring was conducted two weeks after the start date, thus, we did not need to use proxy sites.
The following Table 7 includes the condition at project start for each indicator, and each subsection provides an explanation on how these were calculated.
Each camera trap had a unique name and GPS coordinate to identify which camera captured which animal. Once the raw data was collected and reviewed, it was aligned with the Nature Framework requirements. The data collected at each camera trap could have been associated with a different functional group, so each location was taken and added to the shapefile of the functional group.
Then, this file was cross referenced to the raw data collected in the field according to the camera trap ID and coordinates. If the camera trap was in an area not described as Tropical/Sub-tropical dry forests, the data was excluded, as seen in Table 8.
To calculate the species richness, the total number of species observed in each ejido was added for the Project Extent, while excluding any species that was found outside of the tropical/sub-tropical dry forests.

Aboveground Biomass
To ensure high-precision and replicable values of Aboveground Biomass (AGB), raw GEDI data was used: Spaceborne LiDAR: NASA GEDI Level 4A (GEDI04_A_002)
The Global Ecosystem Dynamics Investigation (GEDI) L4A Monthly dataset was utilized as the primary source for biomass calibration. GEDI provides high-resolution laser ranging observations of the Earth’s 3D structure from the International Space Station. Footprint-level estimates of Aboveground Biomass Density (AGBD) were extracted and used directly.
GEDI’s LiDAR technology provides direct measurements of canopy height and vertical profile, which are more strongly correlated with biomass than passive optical signals alone. This minimizes the risk of “signal saturation” in high-biomass areas.


GEDI_AGB_2023_raw.tif

Download GEDI Aboveground Biomass Raster (2023)



Canopy Cover
The canopy cover raster was retrieved from the NASA hub and the product is described in Step H3. Canopy Cover directly measures habitat structure and is highly sensitive to both ecosystem degradation and conversion.
The indicator has 10 years of continuous historical data, enabling regression-based forecasting as required by SD VISta for using historical conversion rates MODIS is a credible, globally recognized source with documented accuracy and validation Spatial resolution (250m) complies with SD VISta requirement of ≤300m.
The indicator is already used for program monitoring, ensuring consistency between baseline and verification. Compliance with Section 7.5.4: Dataset meets all data requirements including spatial resolution, temporal coverage, credible source, and sensitivity to habitat conversion.

title: Step 6. Calculate Condition at project start (year 0)
text:
The following arithmetic mean equation was followed to calculate the condition of the indicators at project start. \[
C_0 = \frac{\frac{St_1 + St_2 + \cdots + St_n}{n} + \frac{Cm_1 + Cm_2 + \cdots + Cm_n}{n}}{2}
\]
Where:

\(C_0\) = Condition at project start (Year 0)

\(St\) = Standardized structure indicator at project start (year 0) (calculated as SI0 in Step 5)

\(Cm\) = Standardized composition indicator at project start (year 0) (calculated as SI0 in Step 5)

\(n\) = Total number of structure and composition indicators


The calculation can be found below:
\[
\frac{\left(\frac{0.812 + 0.833}{2}\right) + \left(\frac{0.511 + 0.935 + 0.674}{3}\right)}{2}=0.76
\]

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
text:
Step H1. Map Buffer Zone Around Project Extent
The spatial boundaries of the Project Extent were digitally mapped using QGIS and provided as a shapefile. Per the SD VISta Nature Framework v1.0 Habitat Conversion Risk Method, buffer zones around the Project Extent are not required for baseline calculation. The methodology requires baseline calculations only within the Project Extent where conservation activities are implemented and credits will be claimed. Nevertheless, the buffer is mapped in Step 1 of the Crediting Baseline code.
The spatial boundaries of the Project Extent were digitally mapped using the selected Ejidos and the Tropical and Sub-Tropical Forests class from the IUCN global dataset described in Step 1.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H2. Choose structure indicator for modeling
text:
Step H2. Choose structure indicator for modeling
The structural indicator used for the Habitat Conversion Risk method is one of the structure indicators chosen for monitoring, that is Canopy Cover. As such, it is a target indicator, and not a proxy indicator, and as such it did not require quantifying the correlation with its proxy (i.e. the indicator is not a proxy). For a justification as to why this index was used, refer to Table 15.


Table 15. Condition Indicator Employed to Quantify Habitat Loss Risk

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H3. Create or source a raster of the structure indicator
text:
Step H3. Create or source a raster of the structure indicator
A probability raster was generated using regression analysis fitted to 10 years of historical Canopy Cover data. This approach fulfills SD VISta’s requirements to use historical conversion rate for predicting future habitat conditions. This approach employs pixel-level linear regression models that incorporate both spatial and temporal drivers of habitat conversion, allowing for spatially explicit forecasts that reflect observed historical trends.


Table 16. Sources and Justification for Covariates Used for Crediting Baseline












Modeling approach:
The probability raster was generated using a pixel-level regression approach. For each pixel in the study area, a separate linear regression model was fitted to the 10-year historical time series of tree cover. This spatially explicit approach allows conversion risks to vary across the landscape based on local conditions and trends, rather than imposing a uniform average across all areas.
The regression model for each pixel takes the following form: TreeCover at time t equals β₀ plus β₁ times CISI plus β₂ times Year plus ε, where TreeCover is the observed tree cover percentage at time t serving as the response variable, β₀ is the intercept term estimated from the data, β₁ is the coefficient for CISI representing the relationship between infrastructure proximity and tree cover, CISI is the Critical Infrastructure and Societal Index value at the pixel which remains constant over time, β₂ is the coefficient for Year representing the historical conversion rate measured in percentage points of tree cover change per year, Year is the temporal variable , ε is the error term assumed to be normally distributed with mean zero and variance σ², and σ is the standard error of the regression which quantifies prediction uncertainty.
The model is fitted using ordinary least squares (OLS) regression, which estimates the coefficients β₀, β₁, and β₂ that minimize the sum of squared residuals between observed and predicted values. For each pixel, the regression uses 10 data points (one per year) to estimate three parameters, providing seven degrees of freedom for statistical inference, which is adequate for robust parameter estimation.
The coefficient β₁ quantifies the spatial relationship between infrastructure proximity and tree cover. A negative β₁ indicates that pixels closer to infrastructure (higher CISI values) tend to have lower tree cover, consistent with accessibility-driven deforestation patterns. The magnitude of β₁ indicates how strongly infrastructure proximity affects tree cover at that location. The coefficient β₂ quantifies the temporal trend in tree cover over the historical period. A negative β₂ indicates declining tree cover (deforestation), while a positive β₂ would indicate increasing tree cover (reforestation or regeneration). The magnitude of β₂ represents the historical conversion rate in percentage points per year, which is the key parameter for projecting future conditions.
Once the regression model is fitted to historical data, it is used to generate forecasts for the end of the monitoring period, defined as current year plus 5 years. For each pixel, the forecast is calculated by extrapolating the fitted relationship forward in time using the equation: Forecast at t₀ plus 5 equals β₀ plus β₁ times CISI plus β₂ times (Current_Year plus 5). This forecast represents the expected tree cover value if historical trends continue without intervention. Because CISI is static, its contribution to the forecast remains constant. The temporal component, β₂ times Year, drives the predicted change, with the 5-year projection multiplying the annual conversion rate by the length of the monitoring period.


Model Outputs:
The regression analysis generates two distinct outputs, both required for the SD VISta Habitat Conversion Risk Method. These outputs serve different purposes in the baseline calculation methodology and must not be conflated.
The first output is a raster of forecast values, representing the predicted tree cover at each pixel at the end of the monitoring period, defined as t₀ plus 5 years. These values are calculated directly from the regression equation by substituting the future time value. The forecast values provide the magnitude of expected change based on historical conversion rates embedded in the β₂ coefficient. They are expressed in the same units as the original indicator, namely percentage tree cover on a 0 to 100 scale. The forecast values are used in Step H7 of the methodology to project estimated ecosystem condition to the end of the monitoring period. By comparing forecast values to baseline (current) values, the methodology quantifies the absolute change in tree cover expected under the business-as-usual scenario. This magnitude-based approach ensures that the baseline reflects actual predicted changes in ecosystem condition, measured in meaningful biophysical units rather than abstract probability values.
The second output is a probability raster, representing the likelihood that future tree cover will decline below current levels at each pixel. This probability is calculated using the forecast uncertainty (standard error) from the regression model according to the formula: P(decline) equals Φ times (I at t₀ minus Forecast divided by σ), where P(decline) is the probability that future tree cover will be below current tree cover, Φ is the cumulative distribution function (CDF) of the standard normal distribution, I at t₀ is the current tree cover value at baseline, Forecast is the predicted future value from the regression, and σ is the standard error of the regression representing prediction uncertainty.
This formula calculates how many standard errors below the forecast the current value lies, then converts this standardized distance into a probability using the normal cumulative distribution function. If the forecast predicts substantial decline, meaning the forecast is much less than current value, the probability will be high, approaching 1. If the forecast predicts stable or increasing conditions, meaning the forecast is greater than or equal to current value, the probability will be low, approaching. The probability values range from 0 to 1, with values close to 0 indicating low likelihood of decline and values close to 1 indicating high likelihood of decline.
These probabilities are used in Steps H4 through H6 to classify pixels as high-risk when probability is greater than or equal to 0.3, or low risk when probability is less than 0.3, and to calculate the weighted proportion of the project extent facing conversion risk. The probability raster serves as a risk classification layer, identifying where habitat conversion is most likely to occur within the project extent, without quantifying the magnitude of that conversion.


Statistical Justification:
The probability calculation relies on the statistical assumption that forecast errors are normally distributed around the predicted value with mean zero. This assumption is standard in regression-based forecasting and is justified for ecosystem change models where errors arise from natural variability, measurement uncertainty, and stochastic processes that cannot be perfectly predicted. By using the standard normal cumulative distribution function, the methodology converts the forecast error into a probability statement. This probabilistic framework appropriately reflects the inherent uncertainty in predicting future ecosystem conditions. It acknowledges that forecasts are not deterministic but rather represent expected values with associated uncertainty bands. Areas where the forecast strongly predicts decline, meaning the forecast is many standard errors below the current value, receive high probabilities approaching 1. Areas with uncertain or stable forecasts, where the forecast is within one or two standard errors of the current value, receive moderate probabilities. Areas with increasing forecasts receive low probabilities approaching 0.
This approach is scientifically sound and provides a transparent, replicable method for quantifying conversion risk. The probability values are empirically derived from the data itself through the standard error rather than being arbitrarily assigned, ensuring that risk classifications reflect actual historical variability and predictability at each location. Pixels where tree cover has been highly variable historically will have larger standard errors and more moderate probabilities, while pixels with consistent declining trends will have smaller standard errors and more extreme (high or low) probabilities.


Outputs provided:
Two GeoTIFF files were generated and are provided as deliverables for this step.
The first file, forecast_values.tif, contains the regression-based forecast values for all pixels in the study area. The technical specifications are as follows: compressed float32 GeoTIFF format for efficient storage; projection matching the project extent shapefile, specifically WGS84 UTM Zone 16N; no-data value set to -1 for pixels outside the analysis area or with insufficient data; spatial resolution of 250m by 250m which complies with SD VISta’s requirement of no more than 300m; geographic coverage encompassing the reference region and project extent to enable calculations across all required areas; units expressed as percentage tree cover on a 0 to 100 scale; and a single band representing the forecast for t₀ plus 5 years. This raster provides spatially explicit predictions of future tree cover across the landscape, enabling visual identification of areas expected to experience the greatest declines. High-risk hotspots can be identified as areas with low forecast values indicating substantial predicted decline, which can inform program design and prioritization of conservation interventions.
The second file, probability_of_decay.tif, contains the probability of decline values for all pixels in the study area. The technical specifications mirror those of the forecast file: compressed float32 GeoTIFF format; projection matching the project extent shapefile; no-data value of -1; spatial resolution of 250m by 250m; coverage of reference region and project extent. However, the units differ fundamentally. Probability values are expressed on a 0 to 1 scale, where 0 represents 0% probability of decline and 1 represents 100% probability of decline. This raster serves as the primary input to Step H4, where the 0.3 probability threshold is applied to classify high-risk pixels. It provides a risk classification surface that quantifies the spatial distribution of conversion threat across the project landscape. When overlaid with the project extent boundary, this raster enables calculation of the weighted proportion of high-risk area, which directly informs the slope of the crediting baseline according to SD VISta principles.


Compliance statement:
This approach fulfills SD VISta’s requirement to model “spatially explicit probabilities of habitat conversion using historical conversion rates” as stated in Section 4.2.1 of the methodology. The regression-based forecasting methodology satisfies all key requirements of the framework through multiple mechanisms.
First, the approach uses historical conversion rates by fitting regression models to 10 years of observed data. The temporal coefficient β₂ in each pixel’s regression model captures the actual conversion trend over this period measured in percentage points per year, and projecting this coefficient forward embeds historical rates into future predictions rather than relying on expert judgment or literature values. Second, the methodology provides spatially explicit outputs where each individual pixel receives its own regression model, forecast value, and probability value based on local conditions and trends. This spatial heterogeneity allows conversion risk to vary across the landscape, reflecting the reality that some areas face higher pressure than others due to differences in accessibility, land tenure, and historical management. Third, the approach generates probability surfaces as required by SD VISta. The probability_of_decay.tif raster quantifies the likelihood of decline at each location, providing both the magnitude through forecast values and the likelihood through probability values of habitat conversion. These dual outputs enable the risk-based weighting approach specified in the methodology.
Fourth, the methodology incorporates predictors of habitat loss, specifically the CISI covariate representing a well-established driver of deforestation, namely infrastructure proximity. This ensures that forecasts reflect not only temporal trends, but also spatial patterns of conversion risk driven by accessibility and human pressure gradients.
Fifth, the outputs enable the risk-based baseline calculations required in Steps H4 through H11. The probability raster supports risk classification in H4, exclusion zone application in H5, and risk weighting in H6. The forecast raster supports magnitude calculations in H7 through H11. Together, these outputs provide all information needed to calculate a conservative, defensible crediting baseline that approriately reflects both the likelihood and magnitude of expected habitat conversion.
The methodology is transparent, replicable, and scientifically robust. All assumptions are explicitly documented, all calculations are performed using open-source software with version-controlled scripts, and all outputs are provided in standard geospatial formats that can be validated by third parties. This approach can be replicated at verification events by updating the time series with new observations and re-running the regression models, supporting SD VISta’s requirement for dynamic baseline recalculation that adapts to changing conditions over the program lifetime.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H4. Identify areas of high habitat conversion risk
text:
Step H4. Identify areas of high habitat conversion risk
Per SD VISta methodology, pixels with a probability of decline greater than or equal to 0.3 (representing a 30% or greater chance of conversion during the monitoring period) are classified as high-risk for habitat conversion. The threshold of 0.3 was applied to the probability raster generated in Step H3 to identify these high-risk areas. This threshold is explicitly specified in the SD VISta methodology and represents a balance between including areas with meaningful conversion risk while excluding areas with negligible risk, thereby adopting a conservative approach to baseline estimation.
The process for applying the threshold involved loading the StepH3-1_probability_of_decay.tif file from Step H3 and applying a conditional filter where the original NaN pixels were reclassified as -1, pixels with probability values less than 0.3 were set to NaN indicating they are not considered high-risk, and pixels with probability values greater than or equal to 0.3 retained their original continuous probability values. This retention of continuous values is critical because they serve two distinct roles in downstream steps: in Step H6, a binary count determines the proportion of high-risk pixels within the project extent (any pixel with a value greater than zero is counted as high-risk regardless of its exact probability); and in Step H7, the actual probability values (HighRiskProb_p, ranging from 0.3 to 1.0) are used to estimate the magnitude of expected ecosystem condition loss via the formula I_hat = I(t0) × (1 − HighRiskProb_p), ensuring that pixels with higher probability of decline (for example 0.9) produce a greater expected loss than pixels at the margin of classification (for example 0.31).
The resulting raster, stepH4_high_risk_probability.tif, was saved in float32 GeoTIFF format reprojected to EPSG:4326, with NaN as the no-data value for low-risk pixels and -1 reserved for pixels that were originally NaN in the input, spatial resolution of 250m by 250m, and coverage of the project extent. The pixel values in this output range from 0.3 to 1.0, representing the original probability values for pixels classified as high-risk. Analysis of the thresholded raster revealed the spatial distribution of conversion risk across the project extent. The total number of pixels analyzed corresponds to the full project extent area. Among these, the number of pixels classified as high-risk (probability greater than or equal to 0.3) and the percentage of total pixels classified as high-risk were calculated. The mean probability among high-risk pixels provides insight into the average severity of risk in threatened areas, with higher mean values indicating more certain predictions of decline.
This step fulfills SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H4 requirement that “any pixel with a greater than 30% chance of conversion during the monitoring period is considered high risk.” The 0.3 threshold is explicitly specified in the SD VISta methodology and represents standard practice in conservation risk assessment. This threshold strikes a balance between including areas with meaningful conversion risk that warrant conservation attention while excluding areas with negligible risk where baseline projections would be overly speculative. The conservative nature of this threshold ensures that credits are claimed only for protecting areas that face genuine, measurable threats.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H5. Exclude high-risk pixels meeting exclusion criteria
text:
Step H5. Exclude high-risk pixels meeting exclusion criteria
Step H5 removes exclusion zones from the thresholded probability raster produced in Step H4, following the exclusion criteria outlined in Section 7.5.7 of the SD VISta Nature Framework v1.0. Three categories of exclusions are applied programmatically and combined into a single exclusion mask.
The first exclusion is a 10km donut buffer computed around the project zone boundary. The project zone itself is not excluded; only the 10km ring immediately surrounding it is masked out, preventing pixels in the near-project transition zone from influencing the baseline calculation. The second exclusion removes all pixels located beyond a 500km radius from the project zone, restricting the analysis to a geographically relevant area and excluding distant pixels whose conversion dynamics are unlikely to be representative of project-area risk. The third exclusion applies the polygon geometries contained in the Excluded_all.shp file, which captures protected areas, other carbon projects, and any additional areas within the reference region that must be excluded per SD VISta requirements. All three exclusion geometries are reprojected to match the CRS of the probability raster before rasterization.
The combined exclusion mask assigns a value of 1 to excluded pixels and 0 to non-excluded pixels, and is saved as stepH5_HR_exclusions.tif in uint8 GeoTIFF format with spatial resolution and projection inherited from the Step H3 probability raster. The mask is then applied to the working probability array, setting all excluded pixels to zero so they do not contribute to the high-risk proportion calculation in Step H6 or the magnitude estimation in Step H7.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H6. Assess overlap of high-risk pixels with project Extent
text:
Step H6. Assess overlap of high-risk pixels with project Extent
The stepwise calculation proceeded as follows. First, the total number of pixels in the project extent was counted by creating a mask from the project boundary shapefile and counting the number of pixels falling within that boundary.
Second, each pixel within the project extent was assessed as a binary variable: - pixels with a thresholded probability value greater than zero were classified as high-risk (\(HighRisk_p = 1\)) - pixels with a value of zero were classified as non-high-risk (\(HighRisk_p = 0\))
Third, the proportion of high-risk pixels was calculated by summing the binary values and dividing by the total number of pixels in the project extent, following the SD VISta formula exactly:
\[w
=
\frac{1}{P_{proj}}
\left(
HighRisk_1
+
\cdots
+
HighRisk_{P_{proj}}
\right)\]
Where:

\(w\) = Proportion of the Project Extent assessed as high-risk
\(P_{proj}\) = Total number of pixels in the Project Extent (\(p = 1, ..., P_{proj}\))
\(HighRisk_p\) = Binary variable indicating whether pixel \(p\) is high-risk

 The result indicates that the proportion of high-risk pixels is:
\[w = 0.87\]
meaning that 87% of the project extent contains pixels classified as high-risk for habitat conversion during the monitoring period.
This means that approximately 87% of the project extent has a 30% or greater probability of experiencing tree cover decline over the next 5 years if historical trends continue without program intervention.
This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H6 exactly as specified.
The proportion \(w = 0.87\) will be used in Step H10 to scale the crediting baseline according to the spatial distribution of conversion risk.
A program with a higher proportion of high-risk pixels would have a steeper baseline, while a program with a lower proportion would have a less steep baseline, appropriately reflecting the actual threat level faced by each program.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H7. Project estimated ecosystem Condition to end of monitoring period
text:
Step H7. Project estimated ecosystem Condition to end of monitoring period
The requirement for this step mandates that estimated future ecosystem condition must be grounded in observed baseline values and spatially explicit conversion risk, rather than arbitrary projections. The approach used for this program complies with this requirement by applying the SD VISta formula directly to each high-risk pixel within the project extent.
The formula used is:
\[\hat{I}_{p,tx} = \bar{I}_{p,t0} \times \left( 1 - HighRiskProb_p \right)\]
Where:

\(\hat{I}_{p,tx}\) = Estimated absolute structure indicator value in high-risk pixel \(p\) within the project extent after a monitoring period of \(x\) years (\(t_x\))
\(\bar{I}_{p,t0}\) = Observed absolute structure indicator value in high-risk pixel \(p\) within the project extent at project start (\(t_0\))
\(HighRiskProb_p\) = Probability of habitat conversion for high-risk pixel \(p\)

Where  is the estimated structure indicator value at pixel p at the end of the monitoring period tx,  is the observed tree cover value at pixel p at baseline derived from the last year of the MOD44B_TreeCover_2014-2023.tif time series, and  is the continuous probability of decline at pixel p from the thresholded probability map produced in Step H4, ranging from 0.3 to 1.0.
This formula models binary habitat conversion: with probability  the pixel undergoes full conversion and its indicator value drops to zero, and with probability (1 − ) the pixel remains unchanged at its baseline value. The expected value of this binary outcome is  × (1 − ). This approach ensures that pixels with higher probability of decline (for example 0.9) are projected to retain less of their baseline tree cover than pixels at the margin of high-risk classification (for example 0.31), appropriately reflecting the magnitude of conversion threat across the landscape.
The calculation is applied exclusively to high-risk pixels within the project extent — that is, pixels where the thresholded probability is greater than zero, that fall within the project boundary, and that were not originally NaN in the baseline raster. All other pixels receive a value of zero in the output. The resulting raster is saved as stepH7_estimated_ecosystem_condition.tif in float32 GeoTIFF format with projection and spatial resolution inherited from the Step H3 probability raster, a no-data value of 0, and spatial resolution of 250m by 250m.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H8. Calculate difference between predicted and observed values
text:
Step H8. Calculate difference between predicted and observed values
The difference between the structure indicator’s predicted and observed values for each high-risk pixel in the project extent was calculated using the following formula:
\[\Delta I_{p,t_x} = \hat{I}_{p,t_x} - I_{p,t_0}\]
Where:

\(\Delta I_{p,t_x}\) = Predicted absolute change in condition in high-risk pixel p in the project period of \(x\) years \(t_x\)
\(\hat{I}_{p,t_x}\) = Estimated structure indicator value in high-risk pixel \(p\) after the monitoring period, calculated in Step H7
\(I_{p,t_0}\) = Observed tree cover value at pixel \(p\) at baseline (\(t_0\))

Statistics of the delta raster across the 18,214 high-risk pixels within the project extent reveal the spatial distribution of expected decline. The mean delta is −42.90 percentage points, representing the average expected decline across high-risk pixels. The minimum delta is −80.26 percentage points, representing the maximum expected decline in pixels where both baseline tree cover and conversion probability are highest. The maximum delta is −3.64 percentage points, representing the least severe expected decline among high-risk pixels. All delta values are negative, which is consistent with the Step H7 formula: since  is always greater than zero for high-risk pixels, the estimated future condition is always lower than the baseline value.
This step follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H8 exactly as specified. The delta raster, saved as stepH8_delta_high_risk_pixels.tif, provides a spatially explicit map of expected changes, enabling visualization of conversion risk hotspots, identification of priority areas for program intervention, and supporting evidence for verification audits. The delta values will be averaged across the high-risk pixels within the project extent in Step H9 to calculate the mean expected change, which then undergoes area weighting and normalization to produce the final crediting baseline.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H9. Calculate average change across the project extent
text:
Step H9. Calculate average change across the project extent
The average predicted change across all high-risk pixels in the project extent at the end of the monitoring period was calculated by summing the estimated pixel-level change in structure indicator values for all high-risk pixels, then dividing by the total number of high-risk pixels, as in the following formula:
\[\overline{\Delta I_{t_x}}
=
\frac{1}{P_{\text{HRI}}}
\left(
\widehat{\Delta I_{1,t_x}}
+
\widehat{\Delta I_{2,t_x}}
+
\cdots
+
\widehat{\Delta I_{P_{\text{HRI}},t_x}}
\right)\]
Where:

 = Average absolute change in structure indicator for the set of high-risk pixels in the project extent
PHRI = Total number of high-risk pixels in the project extent (p = 1, …, PHRI)
= Predicted absolute change in conditions in high-risk pixel p after monitoring period of x years (tx), calculated in Step H8

The stepwise calculation proceeded as follows. First, delta values from Step H8 were extracted for all high-risk pixels, totalling PHRI = 18,214 pixels. Second, the delta values were averaged: = −42.90 percentage points of tree cover.
The result indicates that the average expected decline across high-risk pixels is 42.90 percentage points of tree cover over the 5-year monitoring period, if historical conversion trends continue without program intervention. This value will be area-weighted in Step H10 using the proportion of high-risk pixels w = 0.87 calculated in Step H6, and then normalized by the reference value to produce the standardized crediting baseline.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H10. Standardize the area-weighted average absolute change
text:
Step H10. Standardize the area-weighted average absolute change
Standardize the area-weighted, average absolute change in the structure indicator using the high-risk proportion of the project Extent and the indicator’s reference value (Sections 7.3.13–7.3.24) as follows:
\[\Delta C_{\text{pre}} =
\frac{\overline{\Delta I}_{t_x} \times w}{R_v}\]
Where:

\(\Delta C_{\text{pre}}\) = Area-weighted, standardized predicted change in condition
\(\overline{\Delta I_{t_x}}\) = Average absolute change in structure indicator for the set of high-risk pixels in the project extent
\(w\) = Proportion of the project extent assessed as high-risk (Step H6)
\(R_v\) = Reference value for the structure indicator

The result is that the standardized predicted change equals ΔC_pre equals -0.0345, or expressed as percentage, -3.45% over the 5-year monitoring period. This represents the risk-weighted, normalized expected decline in ecosystem condition under the business-as-usual scenario.
The interpretation of risk weighting effect is important. The risk weighting parameter w equals 0.642 scales the baseline according to the spatial distribution of risk. This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H10 exactly as specified. The standardized change accounts for two key factors: spatial risk distribution via weighted proportion w, and baseline conditions via reference value normalization R_v. Both components reflect actual program characteristics rather than arbitrary assumptions, ensuring the baseline is specific to this program’s unique context and threat profile.

title: Step 8. Crediting Baseline
section: Step H11. Use annual predicted rate of change to estimate crediting baseline
text:
Step H11. Use annual predicted rate of change to estimate crediting baseline
The Crediting baseline (Bt) was calculated as the ratio between the delta (Change in condition) and time (number of years in the monitoring period), as follows:
\[\hat{\beta}_{\text{pre}} =
\frac{\Delta C_{\text{pre}}}{t_x}\]
Where:

\(\hat{\beta}_{\text{pre}}\) = Annual estimated rate of change (i.e., estimated crediting baseline)
\(\Delta C_{\text{pre}}\) = Area-weighted, standardized predicted change in condition
\(t_x\) = Number of years in the monitoring period

The components are: ΔC_pre equals -0.0345, representing fractional change over the monitoring period, and t_x equals 5 years, the monitoring period duration. The calculation is: β-hat_pre equals ΔC_pre divided by t_x, which equals -0.0345 divided by 5, which equals -0.0069 per year. The result is that the annual crediting baseline equals -0.0069 per year, or expressed as percentage, -0.69% per year. This represents the expected annual rate of decline in tree cover under the business-as-usual scenario. This calculation follows SD VISta Section A1.2 Step H11 exactly as specified. The annualized baseline represents the expected annual rate of habitat conversion, is based on historical conversion rates embedded via regression, accounts for spatial risk distribution via weighted proportion, is normalized to baseline conditions via reference value, and serves as the reference for credit calculation at verification. This baseline of -0.69% per year will be compared to actual observed changes during verification events, with credits awarded for performance that exceeds (is better than) this baseline scenario.

title: Step 9. Monitor project impacts – Ex-Ante Projections
text:
Drivers of Biodiversity Loss
While the program is only seeking validation at this point, the estimated biodiversity outcomes (ex-ante projections) were required not only by the Methodology but also to understand allocation of funds for benefit sharing, and interest for potential buyers and funders. To estimate the biodiversity outcomes of the program, section 8.2 of the VCS Methodology VM0006 was used to quantify “effectiveness” and drivers of biodiversity loss were used instead of drivers of deforestation and degradation. A simile table was used for quantifying program activities effectiveness reducing the identified drivers of biodiversity loss.
Several studies have examined land-use change, biodiversity dynamics, and socio-ecological drivers in and around the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve and the broader Yucatán Peninsula. Using satellite imagery and land use classification, landscape-scale research documented significant land cover change and increasing habitat fragmentation in Calakmul driven largely by agricultural expansion and logging activities (Vester et al. 2007). Parcel-level spatial analyses further demonstrated that deforestation patterns are strongly influenced by socio-economic factors affecting smallholder farmers, with remote sensing and spatial econometric modeling revealing how land tenure systems and household-level decisions shape land-use trajectories within the reserve (Roy Chowdhury 2006). Complementary research using GIS-based land cover comparisons also showed that productive agricultural activities continue to contribute to forest loss, highlighting the importance of sustainable land-use strategies in the region (Špirić, Vallejo, and Ramírez 2022). At a broader regional scale, land-use change modeling has also been used to explore future scenarios balancing biodiversity conservation, traditional agriculture, and ecotourism in protected areas of the northeastern Yucatán Peninsula (García-Frapolli et al. 2007).
Other studies have focused on biodiversity responses and ecological processes within Calakmul. A five-year biodiversity monitoring effort found that climate-related droughts negatively affected local fauna, particularly due to the drying of seasonal wetlands (“aguadas”) that serve as key wildlife water sources (Slater 2019). Research on traditional Mayan agroforestry systems demonstrated that these land-use practices can support biodiversity conservation, especially for bird and plant communities when compared to more intensive agricultural systems (Bohn et al. 2014). Investigations of wetland ecosystems further revealed that human disturbance influences microbial diversity, with microbial sampling and sequencing indicating differences in bacterial community composition between conserved and urbanized areas (García-Estrada et al. 2024). Long-term ecological patterns in the region have also been examined through paleoecological studies, which used sediment cores and pollen analysis to reconstruct vegetation dynamics across the Holocene and showed how climatic shifts have historically shaped ecosystem composition across the Yucatán Peninsula (Aragón-Moreno et al. 2018).
Socio-environmental interactions and conservation planning have also been key themes in the literature. Mixed-methods research combining participatory mapping and interviews demonstrated that climate variability and fire management policies interact to increase challenges for subsistence farmers in southeastern Mexico, contributing to heightened vulnerability in agricultural systems (Monzón-Alvarado and Keys 2016). At the national level, analyses of protected area networks have assessed the effectiveness of conservation strategies in Mexico, finding that although most mammal species occur within reserves, endemic and restricted-range species remain underrepresented, emphasizing the need for complementary conservation approaches outside protected areas (Ceballos 2007). Together, these studies provide important insights into the complex interactions among land use, climate variability, biodiversity conservation, and community livelihoods in Calakmul and the surrounding region.
Table 17 represents the parameters calculated from the numbers that were used in the literature review
Using the parameters from the table above, the proportion that each driver contributed to overall biodiversity loss was determined. The first four drivers of biodiversity loss (agricultural conversion, illegal logging, forest fires, cattle ranching) are all based on their impact on canopy cover (number of forest hectares lost), while the last driver, hunting and poaching, is based off the impact it has on the composition indicators for quantification (species richness). Table 18 shows these numbers and the calculation used to determine the proportion was dividing the number of hectares lost per driver from the total number lost.

section: Implementation of Program Activities
text:
Implementation of Program Activities
The implementation years for each long-term outcome of the program were then calculated using the long-term implementation plan for the 40-year program period and how effective each outcome/activity is at reducing biodiversity loss drivers. The following literature review focused on the effectiveness of conservation and management interventions aimed at mitigating biodiversity loss and strengthening ecosystem resilience. These studies evaluated a range of strategies, including habitat connectivity planning, biodiversity and species monitoring, community-based patrolling, wildfire management, watershed protection, and alternative livelihood programs.
One major focus is improving habitat connectivity in landscapes affected by agricultural expansion and fragmentation. Frameworks for selecting effective connectivity strategies emphasize the importance of restoring ecological linkages between habitat patches to maintain species movement and ecological processes (Cameron et al. 2022). Supporting this approach, studies on connectivity metrics highlight the use of quantitative indicators to evaluate landscape connectivity and guide conservation planning and monitoring, particularly in fragmented ecosystems (Keeley, Beier, and Jenness 2021). Together, these tools help identify priority areas for restoration and protection to maintain functional ecological networks.
Monitoring and enforcement interventions also play an important role in addressing land-use pressures. Patrolling and monitoring systems, particularly those supported by satellite-based deforestation alerts, have been shown to improve the detection of illegal logging and agricultural clearing when combined with training and incentives for local patrol teams (Slough, Kopas, and Urpelainen 2021). In parallel, biodiversity and species monitoring efforts are critical for understanding the mechanisms driving biodiversity loss and tracking conservation outcomes. Advances in wildlife tracking technologies allow researchers to monitor individual animals and identify processes contributing to species decline (Yanco et al. 2025), while coordinated monitoring initiatives at larger scales can improve the detection of biodiversity change and inform global conservation strategies (Navarro et al. 2017).
Several interventions also focus on reducing ecosystem risks associated with wildfire and watershed degradation. Watershed protection strategies aim to strengthen water resilience in landscapes affected by wildfire and degraded water systems, emphasizing the importance of integrated water and forest management (Belongia et al. 2023). Fuel reduction and wildfire containment are additional management approaches that can help reduce wildfire severity and mitigate risks to water supplies and surrounding ecosystems (Gannon, Wei, and Thompson 2020). Establishing baseline understanding of wildfire impacts is also essential, as research shows that wildfire can significantly affect both biodiversity and long-term water supply systems (Bladon et al. 2014). Finally, conservation strategies often incorporate alternative livelihood programs to address underlying socio-economic drivers of environmental degradation by providing sustainable income opportunities that reduce reliance on activities contributing to habitat loss (Roe et al. 2015). Together, these interventions highlight the importance of integrating ecological monitoring, landscape management, and community-based approaches to support long-term biodiversity conservation.
Based on the literature review, the implementation effectiveness for each outcome was estimated. This number was then used to estimate the overall effectiveness of each outcome addressing each biodiversity loss driver. The effectiveness is presented as a single number, thus the total effectiveness for each driver was combined using a simple summed average of the total.
For the implementation of each outcome over the 40 year period, the percentage of activities that would begin to occur in year 1 of the program were estimated and the number of years it would take for the outcome to become 100% implemented Table 19.

\[L_1 + \frac{1 - L_1}{T - 1}\]
where \(L_1\) represents the level of implementation of the project activity in Year 1, and \(T\) represents the total number of years required for the activity to reach full (100%) implementation. This formulation ensured that the calculated implementation level did not exceed 100%.

###Ex-Ante Projections The ex-ante projections were based on the year-to-year effectiveness that was calculated utilizing components of the VT007 Tool. Please find this information in the following sections, Drivers of Biodiversity Loss, Implementation of Program Activities, and Project Activity Effectiveness on Relative Biodiversity Loss Reduction. To calculate the ex-ante projections for the total number of nature credits that the program will produce over the project period, the baseline conditions of the project from the Condition at time T were applied in the quantification calculation below:
\[(E_t×C_t )- [(E_0×C_0 )×(1+(t×B_t ))]\] Where: - \(E_t\) =Extent at Time t - \(C_t\) =Condition at Time t - \(E_0\) =Extent at Time 0 (Project Start) - \(C_0\) =Condition at Time 0 (Project Start) - \(t\) =time of verification - \(B_t\) =Baseline at Time t
This equation calculates the net change in nature credit outcomes by comparing current project performance to a baseline-adjusted starting condition. The first term represents the current state of the project area, calculated as the extent of the project in hectares multiplied by its overall condition score at the time of verification. The second term represents the expected baseline outcome, calculated by multiplying the project’s initial extent and condition at the start of the project and adjusting this value over time using the baseline rate. Subtracting the baseline-adjusted starting condition from the current project state yields the net benefit attributable to the project at the time of verification. The ex-ante projections are based on a quality-hectare framework that integrates project extent, ecosystem condition, and a dynamic baseline. An assumption was made that the Extent would remain static over the crediting period. Although, this is a grouped program and future iterations for ex-ante projections will need to be reassessed when adding new Project Activity Locations to account for the increased Project Extent. The projected changes in outcomes are driven by improvements in ecosystem condition. Condition values increase over time in line with the expected effectiveness of project activities and stabilize once full effectiveness is achieved. Baseline outcomes are represented through a dynamically adjusted baseline that reflects gradual decline in ecosystem condition in the absence of the project. This baseline trajectory is applied consistently across the crediting period using an annual baseline change rate. Baseline values are expressed in quality-hectare terms to ensure direct comparability with project outcomes. Ex-ante project outcomes are calculated as the product of projected extent and condition for each year. Net benefit outcomes are then derived as the difference between ex-ante project quality-hectares and baseline quality-hectares, representing the additional nature benefits attributable to the project. This structure ensures that projected credits reflect only benefits that exceed the counterfactual baseline. Table 13 demonstrates the calculations that were made for the ex-ante projections. The average annual biodiversity outcomes are projected to be 35,142 Quality Hectares (Qha) and a total of 1,405,677 Qha’s.

title: Program Definitions
text:
Project Extent
According to the Framework definitions, Table 1 presents the boundary, extent and Project Impacts.
The delineation of the Project Extent followed a stepwise geospatial approach. A shapefile representing the complete areas under ejido control was used, reflecting lands over which participating communities hold legal ownership and management rights. This dataset served as the foundational boundary for the program. The ejido shapefile was then spatially refined by clipping it to areas classified within the IUCN Global Ecosystem Database, ensuring alignment with recognized ecosystem typologies used in the methodology. The resulting intersected area represents the final Project Extent, within which all project activities are implemented, and biodiversity outcomes are measured. The ecosystem typology for the Project Extent was determined differently than the instructions of the Nature Framework. This is a project deviation that was needed to accurately define the ecosystem typology in the Project Extent. When using the IUCN GET level 3 classifications and tool for the Project Extent (part of the Yucatan Peninsula, Mexico), the data was inaccurate and deficient. For instance, the main function groups that were found in the Project Extent included intensive landscapes like annual croplands, plantations, and urban and industrial ecosystems, as seen in Table 2.
However, these functional groups are not true for the Project Extent, as they are forested areas. Terra Global has worked closely with Amigos de Calakmul, A.C. (ACAC), implementing partner in this area for over a decade, and recently conducted ground truthing for a carbon project in the same area where extensive LU-LC was assessed. These areas are primarily forested and many of the ejidos included in the program are in the buffer zone of the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve, a largely intact Mayan forest remnant home to much of the wildlife and one of the largest populations of jaguars left in the Yucatan peninsula. Thus, the data used to define the ecosystem types of the Project Extent was based on research and literature (Jung et al. 2020). The data was based on the IUCN Global Database that was later created and used for the methodology, so it is still appropriate to use in this case. This data also allowed the program to refine the Project Extent to the nine ejidos that are included in the Program, and it provided much more accurate results.
The main functional group that was found in all of the ejidos was the Tropical/Sub-tropical Dry forests and thickets. While there were several ecosystem types found in each ejido (Map 1), these ecosystem types were excluded due to being arable land, plantations, or urban areas that are not eligible under the methodology. Furthermore, other ecosystem types, like wetlands and sub-tropical/tropical swamps only made up 1.44% and 0.78% respectively, in the largest areas of the ejidos. The CSV file for the different functional groups per Ejido, relates to Map 1, can also be found in Table 3
The file also contains how the other functional groups were excluded and the total number of dry forest per ejido was summed (find explanation in Project Extent Calculations).

title: Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program Workbook
text:
Introduction
The Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program is located in the core and buffer zones of the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve in southeastern Mexico, within the Selva Maya region of the Yucatán Peninsula. The program operates across ejido lands in the states of Campeche, Quintana Roo, and Yucatán—a landscape of exceptional ecological importance that forms part of the Mesoamerican Biological Corridor. This region contains extensive tropical forest ecosystems and provides critical habitat for a wide range of species, including threatened and endangered wildlife such as the jaguar and Baird’s tapir.

title: Step 3. Define reference values for Condition indicators
text:
Mammal Species Richness
For mammal species richness, the inventory includes camera-trap and field-based studies focusing on different mammalian groups within the CBR. These include studies on ungulates documenting six species (Reyna-Hurtado et al. 2015) and broader assessments of large terrestrial mammals across the Yucatan Peninsula, identifying a total of 14 large mammal species (Reyna-Hurtado et al. 2015). Because many studies focused on specific taxa, results were synthesized across multiple sources to produce a comprehensive mammal species inventory (excluding bats), resulting in a reference value of 46 species. All source publications are cited and archived as part of the documented inventory.


Avian Species Richness
For bird species richness, the inventory draws on multiple avifaunal surveys conducted within the CBR. These include a study assessing bird abundance across feeding guilds that identified more than 300 species (Walsh 2018), as well as a comprehensive inventory documenting 403 bird species, which represents the most extensive avian dataset available for the reserve (González-Jaramillo, Martínez, and Esparza-Olguín 2016). These studies were used to inform a conservative reference value of 230 bird species, reflecting expected richness under intact ecological conditions while accounting for methodological variability among surveys.


Herpetofauna Species Richness
For herpetofauna species richness, the documented inventory includes post-2000 studies conducted in the CBR using standardized field methods such as Visual Encounter Surveys and pitfall traps. A key study documented the presence of 20 amphibian species and 69 reptile species, resulting in a total of 90 herpetofauna species under near-pristine conditions (Colston et al. 2015). This study and supporting literature were archived as evidence of the reference condition.
crumbs: 7  Step 3. Define reference values for Condition indicators

title: Step 3. Define reference values for Condition indicators
section: Structure Indicators
text:
Structure Indicators
For structural condition indicators the documented inventory consists of spatially explicit remote sensing datasets and derived statistical analyses. Canopy cover reference values were based on Terra MODIS Vegetation Continuous Fields (VCF) products at 250 m resolution, generated annually using monthly composites of Terra MODIS land surface reflectance and temperature data across all seven spectral bands. Dataset at project start (t=0) was used to calculate the reference value.

Aboveground biomass
Aboveground biomass reference values were derived from a documented inventory of raw GEDI Level 4A Aboveground Biomass Density (GEDI L4A) data, accessed via Google Earth Engine (GEE) using the monthly composites collection (LARSE/GEDI/GEDI04_A_002_MONTHLY). The aboveground biomass density band (agbd, expressed in Mg/ha) was extracted at the native GEDI footprint resolution of approximately 25 m. Monthly granules corresponding to the project start year (2023, t=0) were filtered to the project area and composited into an annual mean composite representative of baseline conditions. Prior to compositing, pixels with non-positive values were masked to exclude fill values and invalid retrievals. The resulting composite was clipped to the project boundary and exported at 25 m resolution in geographic coordinates (EPSG:4326). No additional aboveground biomass datasets were incorporated; GEDI L4A constitutes the sole source for aboveground biomass reference values in this inventory.

Reproducible scripts and summary outputs were archived to enable independent verification:

title: Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program Workbook
text:
Purpose
The program’s primary purpose is to conserve biodiversity and maintain forest ecosystem integrity while supporting the long-term well-being of local ejido communities. The program achieves this by securing forest conservation in ejido territories through legally binding agreements that commit participating communities to protect forests, reduce pressures from deforestation, hunting, and habitat fragmentation, and strengthen ecological connectivity between ejido lands and the Calakmul Biosphere Reserve.


Activities
Conservation activities focus on the protection and restoration of tropical forest habitats, supported by community-based biodiversity monitoring and locally driven conservation practices. The program initially targets the conservation of approximately 126,000 hectares of forest across nine ejidos—Yohaltún, Pustunich, Manuel Crescencio Rejón, Xkanhá, Chanchén, General Francisco Mujica, Chun Ek, Cancabchén, and Felipe Ángeles—with plans to expand to more than 210,000 hectares in future phases.


Communities
By combining forest conservation with community participation and long-term stewardship, the Mayan Forest Biodiversity Conservation Program aims to deliver durable biodiversity outcomes while reinforcing the role of ejidos as key guardians of one of Mexico’s most biologically rich forest landscapes.
This program starts by supporting the ejidos’ communities and the lands they legally own and manage, which form the foundation and primary focus of all biodiversity conservation activities, community outreach and engagement. The ejidos’ shapefile defines these geographic areas of community control and serves as the starting point for biodiversity calculations. These lands represent the full areas the ejidatarios control regardless of LULC and where conservation actions are implemented.

title: Step 9. Monitor project impacts – Ex-Ante Projections
section: Project Activity Effectiveness on Relative Biodiversity Loss Reduction
text:
Project Activity Effectiveness on Relative Biodiversity Loss Reduction
Table 21 describes the overall effectiveness of each outcome of the Program in reducing relative biodiversity loss drivers in the Project Extent. These numbers were based on assumptions made from the literature review on the effectiveness of activities on the identified drivers (see Implementation of Program Activities).


Table 21. Relative Reduction in Biodiversity Loss Drivers Due to Project Activities (Excluding Leakage)





Table 21. Relative Reduction in Biodiversity Loss Drivers Due to Project Activities








The projections in Table 22 estimate how biodiversity condition improves relative to the baseline over time as project activities are implemented and become effective.
The Biodiversity Condition Rate Relative to Baseline represents the expected condition of the ecosystem under the baseline scenario compared to the initial project condition. This value gradually declines over time, reflecting the expected degradation of biodiversity in the absence of the project.
The Net Gain represents the improvement in biodiversity condition attributable to the project compared to the baseline trajectory. It is calculated as the difference between the baseline condition rate and the full potential condition of the ecosystem. As the baseline condition declines, the relative benefit of the project increases, resulting in larger net gains in the earlier years of implementation until the system approaches its maximum achievable improvement.
The Effectiveness column represents the proportion of the total potential biodiversity improvement that has been realized by the project at a given point in time. This value increases gradually during the early years as project interventions are implemented and ecological recovery processes begin to take effect. Effectiveness approaches a maximum level of approximately 53% once the project reaches full operational maturity and ecosystem responses stabilize.

title: Step 9. Monitor project impacts – Ex-Ante Projections
section: Ex-Ante Projections
text:
Ex-Ante Projections
The ex-ante projections of biodiversity outcomes were based on the year-to-year effectiveness that was calculated utilizing components of the VM0006 Section 8.4.
To calculate the ex-ante projections for the total number of nature credits that the program will produce over the project period, the baseline conditions of the project from the Condition at time \(T\) were applied in the quantification calculation below:
\[(E_t \times C_t) - \left[(E_0 \times C_0) \times (1 + (t \times B_t))\right]\]
Where:

\(E_t = \text{Extent at Time } t\)
\(C_t = \text{Condition at Time } t\)
\(E_0 = \text{Extent at Time 0 (Project Start)}\)
\(C_0 = \text{Condition at Time 0 (Project Start)}\)
\(t = \text{time of verification}\)
\(B_t = \text{Baseline at Time } t\)

This equation calculates the net change in nature credit outcomes by comparing current project performance to a baseline-adjusted starting condition.
The first term represents the current state of the project area, calculated as the extent of the project in hectares multiplied by its overall condition score at the time of verification. The second term represents the expected baseline outcome, calculated by multiplying the project’s initial extent and condition at the start of the project and adjusting this value over time using the baseline rate.
Subtracting the baseline-adjusted starting condition from the current project state yields the net benefit attributable to the project at the time of verification.
The ex-ante projections are based on a quality-hectare framework that integrates project extent, ecosystem condition, and a dynamic baseline. An assumption was made that the Extent would remain static over the crediting period. Although this is a grouped program, future iterations for ex-ante projections will need to be reassessed when adding new Project Activity Locations to account for the increased Project Extent. The projected changes in outcomes are driven by improvements in ecosystem condition. Condition values increase over time in line with the expected effectiveness of project activities and stabilize once full effectiveness is achieved.
Baseline outcomes are represented through a dynamically adjusted baseline that reflects gradual decline in ecosystem condition in the absence of the project. This baseline trajectory is applied consistently across the crediting period using an annual baseline change rate. Baseline values are expressed in quality-hectare terms to ensure direct comparability with project outcomes.
Ex-ante project outcomes are calculated as the product of projected extent and condition for each year. Net benefit outcomes are then derived as the difference between ex-ante project quality-hectares and baseline quality-hectares, representing the additional nature benefits attributable to the project. This structure ensures that projected credits reflect only benefits that exceed the counterfactual baseline.
Table 23 demonstrates the calculations that were made for the ex-ante projections. The average annual biodiversity outcomes are projected to be 125,629 Quality Hectares (Qha) and a total of 5,025,166 Qha.

section: Step H2. Choose Structure Indicator for Modeling

For additional justification regarding the use of this indicator, refer to Table 15.
